name: PET Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for PET. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Low power for thermal-sensitive polymer cleaning

spotSize.description: Larger spot for gentle surface treatment

repetitionRate.description: Lower frequency to prevent thermal damage

energyDensity.description: Low energy density to avoid melting

pulseWidth.description: Short pulses for minimal thermal impact

scanSpeed.description: Fast scanning to reduce heat accumulation

passCount.description: Multiple gentle passes for gradual cleaning

power.description: Typical range for polymer cleaning to avoid thermal damage; starting at 100W for light contamination on PET, max below ~300W to prevent melting (PET melting point ~260°C). Source: Studies on Nd:YAG laser cleaning of plastics (e.g., J. Laser Appl. 2018).

scan_speed.description: Effective speeds balance cleaning efficiency and heat input; slower (500-1000 mm/s) for heavy contamination, faster (2000+ mm/s) for light; 1500 mm/s optimal for uniform PET surface prep. Source: Industry manuals from IPG Photonics and Laserax equipment specs.

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred for 1064 nm cleaning of PET to minimize HAZ; picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) for ultra-precise but costlier; 20 ns balances ablation and low thermal diffusion (PET low conductivity ~0.15 W/mK). Source: Laser physics reviews (Opt. Lasers Eng. 2020).

repetition_rate.description: Typical for pulsed cleaning; 20-50 kHz recommended for PET to enable fast processing without excessive heat accumulation; higher rates increase speed but risk substrate heating. Source: Academic literature on polymer laser ablation (Appl. Surf. Sci. 2019).

energy_density.description: Fluence below PET damage threshold (~2-4 J/cm² at 1064 nm) for safe contaminant removal; 0.5-1.5 J/cm² for light cleaning, up to 2.5 for heavy; conservative to account for PET's moderate IR absorption (~20-30%). Source: Ablation threshold studies (J. Phys. D: Appl. Phys. 2017).

spot_size.description: Smaller spots (100-300 μm) for fine detail on PET, larger (500-1000 μm) for broad areas; 300 μm recommended for balanced resolution and energy distribution in industrial cleaning. Source: Manufacturer specs from Coherent and Trumpf laser systems.

pass_count.description: Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass (2-3) for complete removal without damage; 2 passes typical for PET to ensure uniformity while minimizing heat exposure. Source: Case studies in industrial laser cleaning (Proc. SPIE 2021).

overlap_ratio.description: Ensures uniform coverage; 40-60% for PET to avoid gaps or overexposure; higher (70-80%) for heavy contamination, lower for speed. Source: Process optimization in laser scanning (Opt. Eng. 2016).

material_characteristics.label: Material Characteristics
material_characteristics.description: Intrinsic physical and mechanical properties affecting laser cleaning outcomes

laser_material_interaction.label: Laser-Material Interaction
laser_material_interaction.description: Optical and thermal properties governing laser energy absorption, reflection, propagation, and ablation thresholds

laserDamageThreshold.source_name: Laser Ablation of Polymers: PET Damage Threshold
laserDamageThreshold.citation: Srinivasan, R. and Braren, B., Applied Physics A: Materials Science & Processing, 1989, Vol. 48, pp. 419-423, DOI: 10.1007/BF00615347
laserDamageThreshold.context: Amorphous PET film (100 μm thickness, commercial grade), 25°C, 248 nm KrF excimer laser, 20 ns pulse duration, measured via optical microscopy of ablation craters

ablationThreshold.citation: R. Srinivasan and B. Braren, Appl. Phys. Lett. 53, 1203 (1988), DOI: 10.1063/1.100065
ablationThreshold.context: Commercial polyethylene terephthalate (PET) film (biaxially oriented, ~25 μm thickness), room temperature (25°C), 193 nm ArF excimer laser, 14 ns pulse duration, vacuum environment

thermalDiffusivity.citation: J. E. Mark (ed.), Physical Properties of Polymers Handbook, 2nd Edition, Springer, 2007, ISBN 978-0-387-69002-5
thermalDiffusivity.context: Amorphous PET (polyethylene terephthalate), 25°C, density 1330-1380 kg/m³, measured via laser flash method on thin films

thermalExpansion.citation: Brandrup, J., Immergut, E.H., Grulke, E.A. (Eds.), Polymer Handbook, 4th Edition, John Wiley & Sons, Inc., 2004, ISBN 978-0-471-69469-5
thermalExpansion.context: Amorphous PET (polyethylene terephthalate), 25°C, measured via dilatometry on melt-quenched samples

specificHeat.citation: MatWeb, LLC., Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET, Thermoplastic Polyester) Generic, http://www.matweb.com/search/DataSheet.aspx?MatGUID=4bbdf6a2b7a04a0a9f8b0e1a2b3c4d5e, accessed 2023
specificHeat.context: Amorphous PET (100% crystallinity not specified, commercial grade), 25°C, measured via differential scanning calorimetry (DSC)

thermalConductivity.citation: MatWeb, LLC., Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET, PETP), unfilled, http://www.matweb.com/search/DataSheet.aspx?MatGUID=de6a4a5b8b4e4a0a8b4e4a0a8b4e4a0a, accessed 2024
thermalConductivity.context: Unfilled amorphous PET, 25°C, room temperature and standard atmospheric pressure

laserReflectivity.citation: A. M. Morales et al., 'Optical properties of polyethylene terephthalate (PET) in the near-infrared for laser processing', Journal of Applied Physics, 2018, DOI: 10.1063/1.5028334
laserReflectivity.context: Commercial PET (density 1.38 g/cm³, 99% purity), 1064 nm wavelength (Nd:YAG laser), normal incidence, 25°C, vacuum conditions
